news.title: Hong Kong Slides in Global English Rankings

caseStudy.text: In November 2025, the EF English Proficiency Index revealed Hong Kong plummeted (v. 暴跌) to 39th globally with a score of 538, marking a fourth consecutive (adj. 連續的) year of decline. The city now trails (v. 落後於) Malaysia and the Philippines in Asia. Experts ascribe (v. 歸因…於…) the alarming trajectory (n. 軌跡) to pandemic-era disruptions in interactive learning, with youth proficiency particularly sluggish (adj. 遲緩的) despite post-pandemic recovery efforts.

reflections: Falling behind Malaysia and the Philippines undermines (v. 削弱) Hong Kong's historical advantage as Asia's premier English-speaking city, prompting concerns about whether the SAR can maintain its status as a global financial center when regional competitors offer superior bilingual talent pools (phr. 雙語人才庫).

Hong Kong scored 550 in Reading (ranking significantly higher in this metric) versus a dismal 494 in Speaking. This lays bare the local education system's overemphasis on exam-oriented reading and writing at the expense of (phr. 以…為代價) conversational fluency. [HANDWRITTEN: 暴露問題]

The sharp decline among 18–20-year-olds has highlighted the unintended consequences of prolonged (adj. 長期的) online learning, where muted video lessons and masked classrooms eliminated opportunities for spoken practice.

The decline calls for (exp. 呼籲) a pedagogical shift (phr. 教學轉變) away from rote memorization (phr. 死記硬背) and exam drills toward interactive, speaking-focused instruction that prioritizes real-world communication skills over test scores.

Reasons for Hong Kong students' declining English proficiency: diminishing (adj. 減少的) exposure to English outside classrooms due to the dominance of Cantonese and Mandarin in daily life / over-reliance on rote learning (n. 死記硬背) and exam-oriented drilling / reduced opportunities for immersive (adj. 沉浸式的) interactions with native speakers / the proliferation (n. 激增) of short-form social media content in Chinese, which shortens attention spans for long-form English reading / students' reluctance (n. 不情願) to speak English for fear of making mistakes and losing face / the phasing out (phr. 逐步淘汰) of native English-speaking teachers (NETs) in some schools due to budget constraints (n. 預算限制) [HANDWRITTEN: P4 ☆]

news.title: HKUST's New Medical School

caseStudy.text: In November 2025, the government approved HKUST's proposal to establish the city's third medical school. Leveraging (v. 利用) its strengths in artificial intelligence and biomedical technology, HKUST aims to nurture (v. 培養) tech-savvy (adj. 精通科技的) doctors through an interdisciplinary (adj. 跨學科的) curriculum. The school has forged (v. 開創) partnerships with around 40 renowned (adj. 知名的) medical institutions globally.

reflections: The establishment of Hong Kong's third medical school would address critical doctor shortages driven by an aging population, alleviating the strain on the city's overstretched healthcare system. [HANDWRITTEN: 爆<]

Expanding medical training capacity may alleviate (v. 緩解) Hong Kong's chronic understaffing (phr. 長期人手不足) in public hospitals, potentially reducing medical blunders (phr. 醫療失誤) and burnout caused by overworked healthcare professionals.

HKUST's medical school pioneers a paradigm shift (exp. 典範轉移) in medical education by integrating cutting-edge technologies (phr. 尖端科技) into its curriculum. Graduates will be equipped with competencies essential for precision medicine (phr. 精準醫學) and data-driven clinical decision-making (phr. 數據主導的臨床決策), ultimately improving diagnostic accuracy (phr. 診斷準確性) and patient outcomes (phr. 治療成效).

The school's partnerships with about 40 famed institutions, including Peking Union Medical College Hospital and Imperial College London, demonstrate Hong Kong's strategic positioning as a Greater Bay Area medical hub bridging Chinese and Western healthcare systems.

otherExamples.title: AI 放落醫療 [HANDWRITTEN: AI 放落醫療]
points: Breast cancer screening (UK): The NHS launched a massive trial in February 2025 involving 700,000 women to test AI-assisted mammography (phr. 人工智能輔助乳房造影), evaluating its ability to detect subtle (adj. 細微的) tissue changes earlier than human radiologists.
New 2025 trials utilize AI-enhanced predictive modeling to detect acute ischemic stroke (phr. 急性缺血性中風), significantly outpacing conventional clinical assessment.
deep-learning model was validated in late 2025 trials for predicting 6-year lung cancer risk from a single low-dose CT scan, showing high accuracy even in non-smokers.

news.title: Hong Kong's Worst Fire in Decades

caseStudy.text: On November 26, 2025, a catastrophic (adj. 災難性的) blaze (n. 大火) erupted at Wang Fuk Court, a Tai Po housing estate undergoing renovations (n. 翻新工程), claiming at least 160 lives. Flames rapidly engulfed (v. 吞噬) seven residential towers covered in bamboo scaffolding (phr. 竹棚架) and mesh netting (phr. 圍網), with authorities attributing (v. 歸因…於…) the swift spread to substandard (adj. 不合格的) flammable (adj. 易燃的) materials, including styrofoam boards (phr. 發泡膠板). [HANDWRITTEN: ~ inferno, →摩老]

reflections: The tragedy has exposed (v. 揭露) critical failures in building safety oversight (n. 監管). It has also highlighted the dangers of cost-cutting (n. 削減成本) in construction, as investigators probe suspected corruption (n. 貪污) in the tendering (n. 招標) process that may have prioritised profit over safety compliance (n. 安全合規).

The tragedy lays bare (exp. 揭露) a bitter irony: in one of Asia's wealthiest and most developed cities, highly combustible materials (phr. 易燃材料) and non-functional fire alarms caused the deadliest residential high-rise fire in a modern developed city in the 21st century, exposing how prosperity could mask deep-rooted regulatory failures (phr. 監管失效).

The outpouring (n. 湧現) of support – from McDonald's providing free meals to hotels offering rooms and HK$3.7 billion in public donations – reinforces (v. 加強) Hong Kong's reputation as a city of compassion, proving the city remains united in crisis despite social divisions (phr. 社會分歧). [HANDWRITTEN: 同情心, P2☆]
Volunteers' efforts to rescue residents' pets, including cats, dogs and tortoises, reflect evolving attitudes toward animal welfare (n. 動物福利), where pets are increasingly regarded as family members.
otherExamples: 1996 Garley Building fire in Hong Kong (phr. 嘉利大廈大火) / 2017 Grenfell Tower fire (phr. 格蘭菲塔大樓火災) in London / 2003 Daegu subway fire (phr. 大邱地鐵縱火) in South Korea

news.title: Travel KOL Acquires Record-Breaking Luxury Unit

caseStudy.text: (n. 張鎵安 Cheung Lim-on), renowned (adj. 著名的) for teaching credit card rewards strategies (phr. 策略), splurged (v. 揮霍) HK$136.8 million on a North Point Harbour Grand II penthouse unit. The 2,118-sq-ft
unit with rooftop terrace (n. 平台) fetched (v. 售得) HK$64,589 per square foot, shattering (v. 打破) Harbour Grand II's price records. [HANDWRITTEN: ←]

reflections: This record-breaking transaction has drawn attention to how influencer marketing has evolved into a formidable revenue stream, with industry leaders reportedly earning upwards of HK$50 million annually through diversified partnerships. [HANDWRITTEN: 強大/做著者的challenge]
The success story has highlighted how niche expertise (phr. 市場一隅專業知識) can be parlayed (v. 使…產生效益) into substantial wealth in the digital economy.

The case has prompted us to reflect on the widening wealth gap within the creator economy itself, where a handful of (phr. 少量的) industry leaders command (v. 贏得) eight-figure incomes while countless smaller influencers struggle to sustain basic livelihoods.

This conspicuous consumption (phr. 炫耀性消費) might inadvertently (adv. 無意地) inspire younger generations to pursue influencer careers for financial gain rather than genuine passion, potentially saturating (v. 使…飽和) the market with mercenary (adj. 唯利是圖的) creators.
otherExamples: Other famous KOLs / YouTube channels in Hong Kong: Pomato (Comedy sketch 搞笑短片 video production team) / 100Most (Hong Kong pop culture commentary and political satire) / Emi Wong (workout routines and wellness content) / Coffee Lam (fitness and motherhood vlogs) / Martin (creates Cantonese-language cooking tutorials featuring authentic dishes 地道菜式, snacks, and desserts) / Kiki and May (food and travel YouTubers) / Fly For Miles (credit card miles and finance blogger)

phrases.phrase: Among all the + N., the most noteworthy one is that ...
phrases.zh: 在眾多…中，最值得注意的是…
phrases.exampleSentence: Among all the arguments against the removal of PE lessons, the most noteworthy one is that it will put students' health at great risk.
phrases.handwrittenNotes: TS (Topic Sentence marker) | for = 辯駁對手 | Noteworthy = compelling = powerful (跟most) = tenable = soundest (× 跟most()) e.g. the soundest

phrases.zh: 沉迷於 / 令人擔憂的
phrases.exampleSentence: It is distressing that many teenagers are fixated on video games.
phrases.handwrittenNotes: 沉迷於 → 令人擔憂的 | distressing (green highlight)
superReinforcement: Undoubtedly, the phenomenon that students in the city are fixated on exam grades is worrying.
The city is hooked on the new drama series on the bromance (n. 兄弟情) between the two young men.
Teenagers nowadays are so obsessed with online computer games that the vast majority of them have short-sightedness (n. 近視).
On no account should adolescents get steeped in the blind pursuit of brand-name products.
It is not uncommon that parents nowadays are spending ever more money on their children's education, preoccupied with the idea that their children, if taught by the best teachers, will be successful.
The teenager is gripped by her addiction to anime that she cannot stop watching it even for a day.

phrases.zh: 過份重視 / 絕對不低估
phrases.exampleSentence: Parents should by no means attach inordinate importance for their children's academic accomplishments.
phrases.handwrittenNotes: 過份重視 | 絕對不低估 | accomplishments = 態度 (handwritten) | attach ≈ excessive / undue / too much / disproportionate importance to | put/place … emphasis on | give … weight to
superReinforcement: Helicopter parents usually attach inordinate importance to their children's academic success.
Society as a whole puts too much emphasis on economic growth.
It is every girl's dream to be pretty, but nowadays too much emphasis is placed on appearances.
Some people give undue weight to their online persona (n. 表像人格), allowing their social media presence to dictate (v. 支配) their self-image and self-esteem.

phrases.exampleSentence: The grade-fixated education system has exerted tremendous pressure on students.
phrases.handwrittenNotes: 施壓 | exert : put | tremendous : enormous / intolerable / unrelenting / too much / undue (太多) | pressure : stress | exert tremendous pressure = put a great strain on
superReinforcement: Adding an extra test week would definitely exert intolerable pressure on the already stressed students.
Tens of thousands of parents signed a Facebook petition (n. 請願書) demanding the scrapping (n. 廢棄) of the Primary Three exams, saying they put too much pressure on children.
That parents desperately want their children to succeed is understandable, but what is really beneficial to their development is not to exert too much pressure on them.
In order to raise productivity, companies must do more than just place their employees under constant pressure.
If we want to prevent teenage suicide, we should teach teenagers how to cope with tremendous pressure.

phrases.exampleSentence: The ever-rising youth suicide rate in the city speaks volumes about problems with our education system.
phrases.handwrittenNotes: 清楚說明（當e.g.）| problems with : defects in / faults in / imperfections in / shortcoming in | speak volumes about : points to / lay bare / bring to light / indicate
superReinforcement: The mayor's ratings (n. 評分) speak volumes about his popularity – they have remained low ever since the first day he took office (exp. 上任).
The initiative speaks volumes about the government's resolve (n. 決心) in turning the city into a centre of innovation.
The latest audit report (n. 審計報告) has brought to light the high operating expenses of the human resources department (phr. 人事部).
The new biography of the billionaire has made clear that she was a dedicated philanthropist (n. 慈善家) throughout her life.
The vice-principal's sudden resignation has exposed to view how divided the school's top management (phr. 高級管理層) are.
The documentary lays bare the problem of self-censorship (n. 自我審查) in the country.
The new study indicates a strong relationship between bullying and low self-esteem.

phrases.exampleSentence: The exhibition captivates locals and tourists alike.
phrases.handwrittenNotes: 兩者都（不只A還是B）| 吸引 | alike adv | 2) Pet owners and their pets often look alike (adj) | pet owners often look like their pets | = resemble
superReinforcement: The national museum is a popular destination for locals and tourists alike, offering a variety of educational programmes.
The independent bookstore captivates (v. 使…著迷) bookworms and literary critics (n. 評論家) alike as it offers a broad selection of titles to satisfy varied reading interests.
The twin brothers Jack and Josh look alike and have similar mannerisms (n. 習性), causing confusion even among their closest friends and family members.
The replica (n. 複製品) building looks like the original architectural landmark.
For the company, the threat of low-priced look-alike (adj. 相似的) products is not just pestilent (adj. 有害的/討厭的) but potentially lethal (adj. 致命的).

phrases.exampleSentence: Going on a digital detox is a great way to rejuvenate our mind and body.
phrases.handwrittenNotes: 電子排毒 | 換黎-新 | Going on a digital detox : detoxing from the digital world / abstaining from social media / taking some digital down time / unplugging from digital life / get off 'the grid' / 開始 going tech free / embarking on a social media cleanse

synonyms.word: embark on a social media cleanse

superReinforcement: Taking a vacation in the countryside offers urban dwellers (phr. 都市人) a chance to detox from the digital world.
Having realized the harmful effects of excessive screen time, the teenager decided to going on a digital detox.
To reduce distractions and regain a sense of productivity, many students decided to abstain from (v. 戒絕) social media during their exam preparation period.
After a long day of work, most workers found solace (n. 慰藉) in their digital downtime, where they would disconnect from their devices.
Feeling overwhelmed (adj. 不堪重負), the CEO decided to go device-free for a weekend retreat (n. 靜修), leaving her phone and tablet at home.
As more people recognize the negative impact of social media on their self-esteem and well-being, they are making a conscious (adj. 故意的) choice to embark on a social media cleanse.

phrases.exampleSentence: Travelling allows us to take a break from the hustle and bustle of city life.
phrases.handwrittenNotes: 遠離煩惱 | 大難 | take a break from : escape / step away from / break free from / cut ourselves off from / distance ourselves from / detach from / have a respite from → 頭氣 / have a breather from
superReinforcement: The trip would be a wonderful opportunity for students to take a break from their hectic (adj. 忙亂的) schedules and interact with their peers and teachers outside the classroom.
To fully concentrate on writing her novel, the popular author has decided to cut herself off from social events and gathering for several months.
Working parents need to take a breather from their responsibilities once a while (phr. 偶爾), taking part in leisure activities to unwind (v. 放鬆).
In recent years, more and more families like to go glamping (n. 豪華露營) over weekends to escape from the hustle and bustle of city life.
Under peer pressure, impressionable (adj. 易受影響的) teenagers often find it difficult to distance themselves from toxic friendships.

phrases.exampleSentence: There is a close correspondence between early childhood trauma and mental health issues in adulthood.
phrases.handwrittenNotes: 因果 / 密切關係 | ~密切有情 | a close / strong / inseparable / inextricable correspondence / relationship / link / connection / tie / *correlation between … and …
superReinforcement: There is a close correspondence between the prosperity of fast food chains and the rise in the number of overweight children.
There is an inseparable link between prostitution (n. 賣淫) and human trafficking (phr. 販賣人口).
It is known that depression is closely associated with other mental illnesses such as anxiety disorder (phr. 焦慮症), social phobia (phr. 社交恐懼症) and so on.
The rise in the number of overweight children is inextricably correlated with the prosperity of fast food chains.

phrases.exampleSentence: Hong Kong's high-stakes public examinations stifle innovation.
phrases.handwrittenNotes: 風險高的 / 把稅 | high-stakes = high risk / make-or-break / all-important / one shot
superReinforcement: The CEO has decided to launch a new tech product, which was deemed as a high-stakes move for the company.

Some amateur (adj. 業餘的) hikers are not aware of the high-stakes nature of their trails (n. 路徑); even worse, they often attempt to navigate (v. 穿過) treacherous (adj. 變化莫測的) terrain (n. 地帶) and inclement (adj. 惡劣的) weather conditions.
The make-or-break exam has given rise to a multimillion-dollar private tutoring business in the city.
Lack of public space severely affects the city's all-important tourist industry.
The risky investment in cryptocurrency (n. 加密貨幣) has put many investors' savings at stake.

phrases.exampleSentence: The serene village provides city dwellers with a much-needed getaway from their hectic schedules.
phrases.handwrittenNotes: 短假期 / 逃離 | 非常需要 | much needed = badly needed | serene = tranquil | getaway / respite / breather / break from | 鬆一口氣
superReinforcement: She planned a weekend getaway with her best friends in celebration of (phr. 為慶祝…) her birthday.
Drug addicts generally think that taking drugs could bring them a brief respite from the harsh reality.
After months of hard work, many public exam candidates got away for a few days, treating themselves to a short overseas trip.
The Hong Kong Observation Wheel is a perfect weekend getaway (n. 出走旅遊的景點) where you can enjoy fascinating views on a ride there.
Most employees have no alternative but to work overtime, sacrificing their much-needed work-life balance.
After all the studying and exams, the students were in desperate need of (phr. 極度需要) a badly needed respite.

phrases.exampleSentence: Students will benefit considerably from the programme.
phrases.handwrittenNotes: 由…獲得重大好處 | benefit considerably from ≈ tremendously / enormously / substantially / immensely | 40倍獲得 reap considerable benefits from | 2) The programme will benefit student (物) | benefit = be beneficial to / bring ∴ benefit to / will be of benefit to | × passive voice × be benefited from
superReinforcement: Students would benefit considerably from a broader range of elective courses which allow them to explore various interests and potential career paths.
By investing in renewable energy sources, the city reaped substantial benefits from improved energy efficiency and reduced operating costs.
The benefits of having a healthy diet to one's physical health and mental well-being are immense.
Developing effective time management skills is immensely beneficial to students, as it helps them prioritize tasks and strike a healthy work-life balance.
Embracing inclusion (n. 包容) and diversity (n. 差異) at work benefits companies by fostering (v. 促進) innovation and creativity.
Learning a second language can be of huge benefit to individuals, as it opens up opportunities for career progression (n. 發展).
It will be to students' benefit to engage in group study sessions as these can provide different perspectives and deepen their understanding of the subject.

sampleEssays.title: Debate Speech on Policy of Abolishing Students' Class Position
sampleEssays.question: 18 DSE P2 Q.5
sampleEssays.context: You are representing your school at an inter-school debating competition. The motion is: 'The policy of reporting students' class position should be abolished'.

diction.zh: phr. 虎媽與直升機父母/怪獸家長

body.para:
Good morning adjudicators, guests, the opposition side, and students,

I am Chris Wong, the captain of our team. Today, it is my privilege¹ to stand here to argue for the motion that 'the policy of reporting students' class position should be abolished.'

To all the guests and students sitting here in the hall, can you still vividly recall your feelings when you last received your report card with a long list of numbers indicating your position? Did you feel excited? Delighted? Deflated²? Or desperate? Are you satisfied with the fact that your academic results are determined by a set of numbers? While some contend that these numbers are good indicators that drive students to academic achievements, my team holds firmly to the belief that these rankings are merely evils suffocating students and the policy of reporting students' class position should be abolished. My team have three points to make.

annotations.text: 最佳人個假借善意 / Topic Sen

body.para: Among all the demerits³ of this system, the most noteworthy one is that reporting students' class position distorts the genuine purpose of education. Under the current education system in Hong Kong which is fixated on good grades, students attach inordinate⁴ importance to examinations as they believe that passing exams with flying colours would guarantee them a spot in university and thus a prosperous future. This notoriously demanding education system has long exasperated⁵ students, and the system of reporting students' class position has aggravated⁶ that. Just imagine this. After seeing these numbers on the report card, what would your first thought? Probably you will catch a glimpse of the results of other classmates to make a comparison. If one is better than others, they will become complacent⁷ and the desire to maintain this 'social status' will spring up⁸, making them fight tooth and nail⁹ just to get a decent class position. Education would become a ranking game from their perspective, and the genuine meaning of education would be lost. If one loses this game against others, they might fall into a dark abyss¹⁰ and deem themselves to be failures. The system of reporting class positions would therefore merely churn out¹¹ exam machines, rather than upholding¹² the meaning of education – to help students develop their own talent. With this in mind, this system should be scrapped.
highlights: Among all the demerits of this system, the most noteworthy one is that

annotations.text: 最佳人個假借善意 / Topic Sen

body.para: On top of distorting the purpose of education, the system of reporting students' class position is unfair to students, especially the higher form students. As you all know, the NSS curriculum allows students to opt for different electives and the combinations of electives are countless. As each subject may have a distinct mode of assessment, it makes no sense to compare students' class positions based on the average score calculated using the marks of different subjects. I myself is a striking¹³ example of this. As I am keen on humanities¹⁴, I am studying English Literature and Chinese History as my electives. Yet, the scores I obtained in the exams for these subjects were lower than my classmates who take science subjects even if I was the best student in my elective class last year. My class position turned out to be far from excellent. Does this imply that I didn't perform well in my exams? Does this imply others who ranked high were necessarily better than me? Obviously not. Given that students take different electives in senior secondary school, class position does not mean a lot and should thus be abolished.

Here comes our final argument – the system of reporting students' class position will exacerbate the phenomenon of 'Tiger Parents' in Hong Kong, ultimately putting a huge strain on students. Our city is infamous for Tiger Moms and Helicopter Parents¹⁵ who exert massive pressure on their children and desperately want them to excel in extra-curricular activities and exams alike. [HANDWRITTEN: A / B→ 不只A還是B | ∧Both] As parents may not know much about the school curriculum or how well their children perform, the one and only indicator for them to gauge¹⁶ their children's performance would be their class position. According to a research study conducted by the University of Hong Kong, the majority of students agreed that parents had showed dissatisfaction with their class position, and cited parental expectations as¹⁷ a main source of pressure. [HANDWRITTEN: e.j. 清楚說明③] These speak volumes about¹⁸ the fact that disclosing class position to parents may make parents more demanding when it comes to their children's academic results. It is lamentable to see that such trivial¹⁹ numbers on report cards ironically provoke bitter arguments between family members and even impede parent-child relationship. Under this line of thinking, the system should be abolished.

Our dearest opponents would counter our stance, contending that class position forces students to improve their performance in exams and provides an incentive for them to reflect on the problems they have in learning. Such an argument is based on the belief that scores alone are not useful enough as they do not reflect one's performance relative to other students, while rankings are better indicators for students to compare their performances each time. Yet, such a claim is ill-founded²⁰. While it is acknowledged that scores do not reflect one's true ability, I think the opposition team has overlooked the function of grades. Isn't assigning students different grades already enough to reflect their strengths and shortcomings? Is it really necessary to impose an extra burden on students by disclosing their rankings? With this in mind, the opponent's argument does not stand.

To conclude, it is the system of reporting students' rankings that distorts the real meaning of education, that creates gross unfairness, and that triggers the proliferation²¹ of Tiger Parents. Our dear audience, do you want to see our teenagers growing up in such a stressful environment? Our dear adjudicators, as a teacher would you want our future generation to learn in an environment in which utilitarianism²² prevails²³? Our dear fellow students, do you want your success to be judged solely by the numbers on report cards? It is patently²³ obvious that abolishing the system of reporting students' class position is the prerequisite for improving our problematic education system. Today's motion must therefore stand. Thank you!

sampleEssays.title: Letter to Parents: Digital Detox Workshop
sampleEssays.question: Exam Language Focus R4.3-4.4
sampleEssays.context: You are Chris Wong, the class teacher of 6A. Your school is organising a digital detox workshop, where participants can cut themselves off from electronic devices for a certain period of time. You would like to invite parents to join the workshop with their children. Write a letter to parents giving them the necessary information about the workshop, explaining its goals and outlining its details.

diction.zh: phr. 數碼健康/電子排毒

body.para: Dear Parents, I am writing to invite you and your child to take part in a two-day digital detox¹ camp scheduled for next month. This is a wonderful opportunity for parents and students to put down electronic gadgets, spend quality time together and reconnect with each other.

Chief among the reasons why we are holding such a camp after the final exams is that we would like to help parents and students take a break from the online world. According to official figures, Hong Kong has one of the world's highest smartphone penetration rates² at a staggering³ 89.8% for people aged ten or above. For many, it is the first object they grab in the morning and the last thing they look at before bed⁴. Research has shown that there is a close correspondence between the use of social media and anxiety. The human attention span⁵ has also declined sharply over the past decade since the first smartphones came on the market⁶. Hyper-connectivity could bring more harm than good. Through a series of⁷ activities and workshops, the camp aims to help teenagers balance tech time with face time. And your company and participation are of the utmost importance in guiding and supporting them.

In addition to shunning the realm⁸ of social media, we think it would be great if you could spend some uninterrupted⁹ time with your child before their biggest challenge to date¹⁰ – the DSE exams. The majority of students in our class say they are highly stressed, the main source of that being the possibility of not getting the desired results in the high-stakes¹¹ university entrance exam. A weekend getaway¹² in early March, where our students can take time out of their gruelling¹³ schedule to play games, watch full-length movies and have long conversations with their family and friends, is a much-needed breather¹⁴. We believe you would benefit considerably from it too. As parents, we all know how hard it is to juggle¹⁵ our jobs, our children's needs and our housework. It is time we gave ourselves some spaces to slow down, unbreak our brains and cement the relationship with our younger generation.

annotations.text: 路途 | 高風格 | 非常需要

body.para: With the above reasons and goals in mind, I am glad to announce that the digital wellness camp, co-organised with the non-profit group

, will take place from 10 am on Saturday, March 9th, to 6pm on Sunday, March 10th, at the Wu Kwai Sha Youth Village. The school will arrange a shuttle bus to take the class to and from the venue that weekend. With the generous support of our school board, the fee for each family is only HK$900, covering meals, refreshments, workshop materials, a night's stay and all miscellaneous¹⁶ charges including the entry fee and transportation. Given the limited number of rooms, each student will only be accompanied by one parent or guardian¹⁷. After the camp, each student will need to make a short video sharing their experience and saying a few words to their parents. The best five clips will be posted on our school's website.

We believe that the camp will be a good time for students to interact with their classmates and parents outside the classroom, and to learn how to thrive in real life, not just in their digital lives. It is sincerely hoped that you will join with your child to participate in this meaningful activity. Please submit the reply slip attached¹⁸ to the general office on or before February 15th. The fees will be collected at a later time. If you have any questions, please do not hesitate to contact me at 3345 6789. Yours faithfully, Chris Wong 6A Class Teacher Hong Kong College
